entry.title: ABAP Cloud dereferencing REF TO DATA into DATA is not working for not released data types
entry.content:
I faced one behavior in BTP ABAP Cloud, which I find weird. Maybe someone can bring more info on the topic.

Imaging we have:

  • software component A - generic tool for something, and can work on generic data type
  • software component B - want to use the tool from A

If I do the tool to accept this data as data ref REF TO data, and this assignment fails in case B sends data ref to not released type (without released API contract).

ASSIGN data_ref->* TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<data>).
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
  RETURN.
ENDIF.

Even this one fails, assignment just returns sy-subrc 4.

FIELD-SYMBOLS <data> TYPE data.       
ASSIGN data_ref->* TO <data>.         
IF sy-subrc <> 0.                     
  RETURN.                             
ENDIF.

But when passing just TYPE data, e.g. via CHANGING, the tool can work fine, and can even change this data in dynamic way.

What is difference here? Why reference cannot work? On first look it doesn't bring any dependency, so why first variant via reference cannot work with not released type generically.

I was trying to find info on that WHY, but without success. Found only confirmation of my understanding that de-referencing is not working in software-heroes blog https://software-heroes.com/en/blog/abap-cloud-reusable-components.

entry.title: Re: Custom Validation not working for create incoming inv...
entry.content:
Dear @kent_2804,

The previous analysis was related to displaying a custom validation message for the invoice amount threshold greater than in the above apps. For those scenarios the relevant extension points were confirmed as MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD for the supplier invoice apps and FIN_ACDOC_VALIDATION for Create Incoming Invoices / FB60. Regarding your additional question about Park Incoming Invoice, I would like to clarify the exact action you are referring to because this is a different scenario

In the Park Incoming Invoice / MIR7 the available actions are:

  • Save Parked Document
  • Hold
  • Save as Completed
  • Cancel

For example I reproduced the Save Parked Document scenario in my environment. This action is handled by MRM_CHECK_INVOICE_CLOUD using:

IF action = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_action_park.

APPEND VALUE #(
messagetype = if_ex_mrm_check_invoice_cloud=>c_messagetype_error
messageid = 'MRM_BADI_CLOUD'
messagenumber = '001'
messagevariable1 = 'TEST BADI ON MIR7 WHEN CLICK SAVE PARKED DOCUMENT'
) TO messages.

ENDIF.

With this logic the message was displayed in the Message log when clicking Save Parked Document

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Credit Check not Block Deliveries
entry.content:
Why one BP shows delivery values and the other does not is because

Credit exposure categories
In UKM_BP → Credit Segment → Credit Exposure, the system aggregates values by category (Sales Orders, Deliveries, Billing Documents, FI-AR, etc.). If you see delivery values for BP1 but not for BP2, it means the delivery for BP2 was not updated into the credit exposure tables (UKM_TRANSFER / UKM_ITEM). This usually happens if the delivery did not trigger the credit check update (e.g., missing credit segment assignment, incorrect risk class, or the delivery type not flagged for credit relevance).

Order vs. Delivery relevance
You mentioned orders are statistical only (not consuming credit). That is fine. But deliveries must be flagged as credit‑relevant in customizing (OVAD / OVA8 settings for delivery types). If one BP’s delivery is not appearing, the issue is not with the order but with the delivery posting into credit exposure.

Custom transaction calling credit check FM
When you run your custom transaction, it calls the credit check FM (likely UKM_CHECK or SD_CREDIT_CHECK). For BP1, the delivery was already posted into credit exposure, so the FM sees it and blocks correctly. For BP2, the delivery was not posted, so the FM only sees the new delivery you are checking, not the cumulative exposure. That’s why the block only happens on the second delivery.

Check the below areas

  • Delivery type customizing: Ensure the delivery type used for BP2 is marked credit‑relevant in OVA8.
  • Credit segment assignment: Confirm BP2 is assigned to the same credit segment as BP1 in UKM_BP.
  • Update to credit exposure: Check table UKM_ITEM after creating the delivery. If no entry exists for BP2’s delivery, the update routine is missing.
  • Risk category / status: Verify BP2 has a valid risk category and credit control area assignment. If missing, exposure won’t update.
  • User exit / BAdI logic: If you have custom logic in exits like EXIT_SAPFV45K_001 or BAdI UKM_TRANSFER, ensure it doesn’t filter out BP2’s deliveries.

entry.title: Clarification on SAP Standard Approach for Parallel Release Cycles in ChaRM
entry.content:
We have two parallel release cycle running in ChaRM. Cycle "A" is set to Build and cycle "B" is set to Scope status.
We have a situation where Release cycle "A" go-live is postponed and due to this its overlapping with cycle "B". Project team is requesting to release transport request held on cycle "B" but since the cycle is in scope status this is not possible. There is also possibility in next 3 months that transports in cycle "B" could move to SIT/UAT system.

Please advise if successor release cycle is in build status then what is the risk here?

What are our options to go forward with this? What are the risks involved and SAP best practices.

Thank you

entry.title: Storage location in planned order limited to MRP area location
entry.content:
Hello everyone, 

I have a question regarding storage location in planned order / fabrication order.  

My case is that I have a planned order in plant 0001 in location S01A and I want to change from where my material is producted. 

I will change the production plant to 0002 and the storage location to S02A (storage location for my plant 0002). The issue is that cannot change the storage location to a location not from 0001 plant. 

I have an error message " Entry 0001 S02A does not exist in T001L" which is normal because S02A is not a storage location for 0001 plant. 

Is there an other way or customizing, that would make it possible to select other storage location in this field ? 

Thank you in advance for your answers. 

Best regards, 

Claire LH

entry.title: Re: Can you create a MILL_OC Combined Order with a single...
entry.content:
Hello Ann,

I discussed this with our product & development team. I understand that this question mainly is about how simple you can integrate with your deckling system vs whether we can bend the concept of order combination in the direction to combine only one order.  Which is not really an order combination at all.

So, the standard answer on this one is: No, this is not an order combination and is not supported with MILL_OC functionality.  As an ex-developer, I would suggest to rather catch this special case/exception in a custom wrapper around the API. 

Best regards,

Stefan Weisenberger

entry.title: Using Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities - Including Project Stock
entry.content:
Contents

  1. Background - Why Standard Maintenance Orders Are Sometimes Not Enough
  2. The Standard Investment Project Process (Recap)
  3. Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities
  4. Adding Project-Specific Stock to the Scenario
  5. Variant: Investment Project Without an Investment Profile
  6. Key Takeaways
  7. Further Reading
  8. System Example Showing the Process Flow


1. Background - Why Standard Maintenance Orders Are Sometimes Not Enough

When a company plans a significant maintenance activity, an overhaul of a complex machine, a major refurbishment of production equipment, or a large infrastructure repair, the cost and material management requirements quickly exceed what a simple overhead project can provide comfortably.

Key challenges in these scenarios include:

  • Tracking all incurred costs (internal labor, external services, materials) in one structured object across a potentially multi-month timeline.
  • Ring-fencing materials ordered specifically for this activity so they cannot accidentally be consumed by other production or maintenance tasks.
  • At the end of the activity deciding which portion of the costs should be capitalised to the fixed asset and which should be expensed. (As a big overhaul will increase the value of the asset, capitalising at least part of the maintenance costs makes totally sense.)

Investment projects in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud address all three challenges in a single, coherent setup.

 

2. The Standard Investment Project Process (Recap)

Before focusing on the maintenance use case, it is worth recapping the standard flow because the maintenance scenario builds directly on it.

In a standard investment project, costs for the creation of a new own-used asset are collected on a project and settled to an Asset under Construction (AuC). This settlement to the AuC can be done event-based or period-based. This moves costs off P&L into the balance sheet. Once the asset is ready for use, the AuC is settled to the final fixed asset. Any costs that cannot be capitalised are redirected to a cost center or an overhead project.

  1. Create Investment Project & WBS Structure
    Define the project with an investment profile. The investment profile drives the automatic creation of an Asset under Construction (AuC).
  2. Collect Costs on the Project
    Post actual costs: purchase orders, goods receipts, time confirmations, service entry sheets. All costs land on WBS elements.
  3. Settlement to the AuC
    At period end (or event-based, triggered by every posting to the project), costs are settled from the investment project to the AuC. This removes costs from P&L and places them on the balance sheet.
  4. Final Settlement to the Fixed Asset
    When the asset is complete, the AuC is settled to the final fixed asset. Non-capitalisable costs are distributed to a cost center or overhead project according to the settlement rule.
  5. Asset Activation & Deprecation
    The asset is activated in Asset Accounting and begins regular depreciation according to the assigned depreciation key.

SAP Community Blog Reference
For a detailed walkthrough of the standard settlement process, see the SAP Community blog post: 
Settlement of Investment Projects in SAP Cloud ERP at community.sap.com.

 

3. Investment Projects for Larger Maintenance Activities

Investment projects are not limited to the creation of entirely new assets. They are equally well-suited for larger maintenance activities. For example, a complete overhaul of a turbine, a production line rebuild, or a major refurbishment of a building; where a (substantial) portion of the incurred costs is expected to be capitalised to the existing fixed asset.

Typical Scenario

A company plans an extensive overhaul of a complex machine. The activity is expected to take several weeks, involve multiple vendors and internal teams, and require a large volume of specific spare parts. The total cost is expected to be significant, and management expects that a meaningful portion will extend the useful life or increase the value of the machine - qualifying for capitalisation.

Why an Investment Project Fits

  • Structured cost collection: WBS elements allow costs to be organised by work package/ project phase (e.g., disassembly, repair, reassembly, testing).
  • Flexible settlement: At the end of the overhaul, the settlement rule determines which percentage is posted to the fixed asset (capitalisation) and which goes to a cost center or another cost object (period expense). Both receivers are optional.
  • Audit trail: with event-based settlement all postings are traceable back to their origin - purchase orders, time entries, material consumptions - via the project.

INFO: When to Capitalise Maintenance Costs
Under IFRS and US GAAP, costs that extend the useful life of an asset, increase its capacity, or improve its quality beyond the original standard are capitalisable. Routine repairs that merely maintain the current condition are expensed. The settlement rule in SAP lets you split both in a single settlement run.

Setup Considerations

When the goal is to settle a portion of costs to an existing fixed asset (not create a new one), you have two options:

  • With investment profile — an AuC is created automatically. Costs flow from the project to the AuC during the activity, and finally from the AuC to the existing fixed asset. This approach is preferred when the activity runs across multiple periods and interim balance sheet capitalisation is required.
  • Without investment profile — no AuC is created. Costs accumulate on the project and are settled directly to the existing fixed asset at the end. This simpler variant is covered in Section 5.

 

4. Adding Project-Specific Stock to the Scenario

For large maintenance activities, materials are often ordered weeks in advance and stored in the warehouse. Without further measures, these materials sit in unrestricted stock and can be issued to any other order or project creating the risk that critical spare parts are consumed elsewhere, delaying the overhaul.

Project stock solves this by ring-fencing materials for a specific WBS element. Any goods receipt for a purchase order referencing that WBS element lands in project stock, not in unrestricted stock. Goods issues from that stock are only possible against the same project.

Availability in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
In SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, project stock is available for investment projects and projects with revenue. This is a deliberate design: overhead cost projects do not support project stock in the public cloud edition.

End-to-End Material Flow with Project Stock

  1. Create Purchase Order with Account Assignment to WBS Element
    The buyer creates a purchase order for the required spare parts. The account assignment references the relevant WBS element of the investment project. The account assignment category is set to Q – Project – Make-to-Order.
  2. Goods Receipt into Project Stock
    When the materials arrive, the goods receipt posts them into project stock (a separate stock segment linked exclusively to the WBS element, special stock indicator Q). The materials are visible in inventory management but are reserved for this project only.
  3. Goods Issue from Project Stock to the Project
    When the materials are needed during the overhaul, they are issued from project stock to the WBS element. The cost hits the investment project directly at actual cost.
  4. Settlement of Material Costs Together with Other Project Costs
    Material costs posted to the WBS element are settled along with all other project costs (labor, services) to the AuC, or directly to the fixed asset, or to other receivers like e.g. cost centers, or to a combination of all of these objects. This can be done using the same settlement rule.

5. Variant: Investment Project Without an Investment Profile

Not every maintenance capitalisation scenario requires the interim step of an Asset under Construction. If the maintenance activity is relatively self-contained and management is comfortable settling directly to the existing fixed asset at completion, an investment project without an investment profile is a simpler and equally valid option.

How It Works

Without an investment profile assigned to the WBS element, no AuC is created automatically. Costs are collected on the project throughout the activity. At the end, the settlement rule points directly to the existing fixed asset (for the capitalisable portion) and to a cost center (for the expensed portion). The settlement is executed once at project close.

INFO: No AuC — But Still an Investment Project
The project must still be created as an investment project (not an overhead cost project) to be eligible for project stock. The absence of an investment profile means no AuC is created, but the project type remains "investment," which is the prerequisite for using project-specific stock in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

When to Use This Variant

  • The maintenance activity is completed within a single fiscal period or a short time frame where interim balance sheet capitalisation is not required.
  • You do not need to report work in progress on the balance sheet during the activity.
  • You want to minimise the number of settlement postings and avoid managing an AuC asset master.

Scenario Comparison at a Glance

Aspect

With Investment Profile (AuC)

Without Investment

Asset under Construction created?

Yes — automatically from investment profile

No — no AuC master record

Interim balance sheet capitalisation?

Yes — costs move to balance sheet at each settlement or even event-based

No — costs remain on the cost object until project close

Settlement target

AuC → final fixed asset at project completion

Directly to the existing fixed asset or other receivers at project close

Project stock eligible?

Yes

Yes — investment project type is still required

Partial capitalisation possible?

Yes — via settlement rule percentages

Yes — via settlement rule percentages

Best suited for

Multi-period projects, large capex activities, new asset creation

Shorter maintenance activities, direct asset improvement, simpler setup

 

6. Key Takeaways

  • Investment projects in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud are not restricted to new asset creation, they are an effective tool for managing costs of larger maintenance and refurbishment activities.
  • Project stock is available for investment projects (and revenue projects) in the public cloud edition. This makes the investment project the right vehicle when material ring-fencing is required for a major maintenance task.
  • The settlement rule gives you the flexibility to capitalise a portion of maintenance costs to the existing fixed asset while expensing the remainder to a cost center. It is also possible to expense 100% of the maintenance costs. This can be done in a single settlement run.
  • If interim AuC-based balance sheet capitalisation is not required, an investment project without an investment profile simplifies the setup while retaining all cost-collection and project-stock capabilities.

7. Further Reading

  • SAP Community Blog: Settlement of Investment Projects in SAP Cloud ERP
  • SAP Help Portal: Scope Item 35F — Project Control: Capital Projects (Best Practices)

 

8. System Example Showing the Process Flow

1. Create Capital Investment Project
when creating the project, you need to use the Project Profile: Investment Project,
otherwise the functionality described in this blog will not be available

The assignment of an investment profile is optional.

  • With an investment profile assigned the system will automatically, with release of the project, create an asset under construction (AuC), you will then periodically or event-based settle the costs from the project to the AuC (move costs out of P&L and capitalise them in the balance sheet).
  • If no investment profile is assigned the system will not create an asset under construction. All the costs posted to the investment project can later be settled to a fixed asset, a cost center, another project,…

2. Capture Planned Costs and/ or Project Budget
If needed, you can upload planned costs or budgets to the investment project.
You can use the app Import Financial Plan Data (F1711) to upload planned costs or project budget.
Planned costs and budgets are differentiated via the plan category. Make sure you choose the category BUDGET01 for the budget upload.

3. Create Demands
If needed, demands can be planned for the investment project. Details are described in scope item 4RF- Project Control – Resourcing and Procurement

4. Purchase Materials into Project Stock

  • create a demand (as described in 3.), or
  • directly create a purchase order (app Manage Purchase Orders) with
    - account assignment category Q – Project – Make-to-Order
    - enter the WBS that should own the stock (item details), you do not need to enter a GL account
    The special stock indicator Q - Project Stock is derived automatically from the WBS account assignment.

    If the purchase order needs an approval, check the tab “Approval Details” to find the assigned approver.
    Approvals can be found via Self Services > My Inbox > Approve Purchase Order

5. Goods Receipt for Project Stock

  • Use the app Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document (F0843) to post a goods receipt into project stock.
  • Check your project reporting (Project Financial Booklet Booklet – Enterprise Projects) to see the balance sheet posting (material inventory) is assigned/ attributed to the investment project
    The project report shows the inventory account (BS) is account assigned to the project and therefore visible in project reporting. This is only done for project stock.
  • Check your material inventory to see the posted material quantity update in project stock (special stock indicator Q).
    This means, these materials are not freely available but can only be consumed by the assigned WBS element.
    app Stock – Multiple Materials (F1595)

6. Consume Material from Project Stock
Use the app Post Goods Movement (MIGO) to consume the material from project inventory to the investment project.
- Movement Type 221
- Special Stock Indicator Q
enter your WBS element as this project stock can only be consumed for the assigned WBS
the project report shows the consumption:

7. Post Costs to the Investment Project
e.g. activity allocation posting (app Manage Activity Allocation)


8. Maintain Settlement Rules
the settlement rule can be maintained in the project master (app Project Control – Enterprise Projects)

In this example 50% of the costs are capitalised and therefore settled to a fixed asset, the other 50% are settled to a maintenance cost center.
It would also be possible to settle 100% to cost center or other cost objects. There is no need to settle to a fixed asset.

9. Project Settlement
app Run Settlement – Actual

entry.title: Convert both static and dynamic content on S4HANA UI and Fiori screens
entry.content:
Hi,

I have a following customer requirement : customer wants a capability to convert the standard text content on S4HANA UI and Fiori screens from English to Indonesian. In addition to the static field and label text, the requirement is to also convert the dynamic text content to the target language.

I have seen a few community blog posts on the usage of SAP Translation Hub to perform the static message / label conversion for S4HANA, where the translated text is pushed back to the S4HANA system.

How can we  achieve a similar capability for the dynamic text information that is loaded from the HANA database to the SAP UI and Fiori screens ? Can this be achieved through SAP Translation Hub or some other mechanism needs to be thought of ?

Thanks in advance for your response and guidance.

entry.title: Clarification on SAP Build Process Automation Pricing – Scheduled Report Downloads
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We use SAP Build Process Automation to automate downloading of 11 reports on a schedule and transferring them to another server. The automation runs unattended and is triggered using SBPA's built-in scheduler. All 11 reports are set up as separate applications inside one automation process.

We have the following licenses in our subaccount (Watertec-NonProd):

Automation-unattended plan

We need to understand exactly how usage is measured and billed for this kind of automation. Specifically:


What is the actual unit of consumption for running an unattended automation — is it per execution/run, per minute of runtime, per screen/step performed, or something else?

  • Since our unattended plan has both a RISE (Subscription) and CPEA (Consumption) entitlement source, how do these two interact? Is there a fixed number of runs/minutes included under RISE before it starts drawing from CPEA consumption?
  • If we run this automation daily (11 reports each time), how would that translate into actual consumption/cost under our current entitlements?
  • Does the number of applications/steps within a single automation process affect consumption, or is it purely based on total execution time/runs regardless of how many steps are inside?

    Regards,

    kanakaraj.M

entry.title: PR Email Notification Issues Incorrect Deadline Date/Time & Missing Decision Note in Rejection Email
entry.content:
We are facing two issues with the standard SAP email templates used for Purchase Requisition (PR) and Purchase Order (PO) approvals and would appreciate your help.

Issue 1: Incorrect Deadline Date and Time in Email Notifications

We have noticed that the approval deadline shown in the email notification is displaying zeros instead of the actual due date and time.

Example:

  • PR created on 07-Jul-2026 at 2:00 PM
  • Approval deadline configured for 2 hours

Based on this, the email should show the deadline as 07-Jul-2026 at 4:00 PM.

However, the deadline field in the email is showing an incorrect value (all zeros) instead of the calculated due date and time.

Has anyone faced a similar issue? Is there any standard configuration or data source that controls this value in the email template?

Issue 2: Decision Note Not Available in PR Rejection Emails

When an approver approves or rejects a PR or PO, SAP provides two fields:

  • Decision Reason
  • Decision Note

For PO rejection emails, both the Decision Reason and Decision Note are included in the email notification.

However, for PR header-level rejection emails, only the Decision Reason is available in the standard email template. The Decision Note entered by the approver is not displayed.

We would like to understand:

  • Where is the Decision Note stored for PR header-level approvals/rejections?
  • Is this information available in any standard CDS view, workflow container, or business object?
  • Has anyone successfully added the Decision Note to the PR rejection email notification?

Any guidance, examples, or suggestions would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Central Gen AI is not executing most SQL queries correctly
entry.content: The Gen AI-assisted database administration feature in SAP HANA Cloud Central is not executing most SQL queries correctly. The majority of queries submitted via the Gen AI virtual assistant are returning errors. This appears to be related to the feature being under active development, as noted in SAP documentation. [1] Only a limited subset of operations — specifically creating an instance and migrating a server — are functioning as expected. All other SQL generation and execution attempts are failing, significantly limiting the usability of the Gen AI feature for day-to-day database administration tasks. [2]

entry.title: Re: How to extract multi references from XML Bank Statements
SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #41 (July 2026)
entry.content:
Summer is here and it’s getting hot. I’m not just talking about the weather. AI is heating up both for the companies that create the large language models and everyone trying to keep up with the latest innovations. All while managing their token costs. SAP is embracing AI for our internal application development and the tools we provide customers and partners for building their own applications.

Sapphire produced several major announcements around our development tools, the biggest being Joule Studio 2.0. Joule Studio 2.0 is SAP’s AI-first development environment for building custom AI agents, apps, and workflows. Powered by the SAP Business AI Platform.

As the development landscape shifts, we want to know what’s important to you. We are interested in how well SAPUI5 meets your needs today, which UI experiences you expect to dominate enterprise apps in the coming years, and which capabilities matter most for your future AI-powered development work. Please take this 5-minute survey.

If you want to join other developers to discuss various traditional and AI-powered Fiori development-related topics from the comfort of your office or home, I encourage you to join our monthly SAP Fiori development roundtable. Register online or email me for an invitation.

Development News

Optimizing SAP Fiori Elements for Mobile Phone Users

While SAP Fiori elements applications are responsive, they adjust controls based on screen size and device type, it does not provide an optimized experience for users. We recently reimagined the SAP Fiori elements mobile experience to include native mobile feel, intuitive navigation, and smarter mobile-optimized layouts. Read more.

Develop RAP-Based SAP Fiori Applications in Minutes with SAP Fiori Tools AI Project Accelerator

Developing enterprise SAP Fiori applications using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a standardized way to build modern, cloud-ready applications. These applications are typically developed in environments such as SAP BTP ABAP Environment and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (version 2602 or higher). The Project Accelerator now supports RAP-based projects. It is available in SAP Business Application Studio under an SAP Build Code subscription. Learn more.

TypeScript 6 and 7 – What UI5 TypeScript Developers Need to Know in 2026

TypeScript is going through its biggest transition in years — and if you build UI5 applications with TypeScript, here we have all the details you need to know. Read about it in this blog post.

UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents: Now Supporting GitHub Copilot

The UI5 Plugins for Coding Agents — previously known as UI5 Plugins for Claude — are now also available for GitHub Copilot. The plugins bring up-to-date SAPUI5 and OpenUI5 knowledge directly into your AI-assisted development workflow, whether you work with Claude Code or GitHub Copilot. Version 0.1.3 also introduces three new skills covering UI Integration Cards, OPA5 integration tests, and OpenUI5 tables. Read more.

Introducing the UI5 Modernization Plugin

Keeping your SAPUI5 or OpenUI5 app up to date just got easier. The new UI5 Modernization Plugin automates the most time-consuming parts of app modernization: replacing deprecated APIs, eliminating global namespaces, updating manifest.json and Component.js, and getting your app ready for Content Security Policy compliance. The plugin is available as a Claude Code plugin and in the GitHub Copilot marketplace. Read the full walkthrough.

 What's next for UI technology in the age of AI?

We're running a short survey to collect input from developers, architects, and UI technology enthusiasts in the SAP ecosystem — and we want to hear from you. Topics include how well SAPUI5 meets your needs today, how AI tools work with your current UI stack, and which capabilities matter most for your future development work. Shape the SAPUI5 road map by answering few questions in just 5 minutes, open July 1–19. Take the survey.

Joule Studio 2.0 announced at Sapphire

SAP announced its latest development environment for building apps, agents, and workflows. Read how it changes how you build apps.

SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI capabilities including agentic capabilities is now available!

ABAP AI in SAP Joule for Developers include agentic capabilities and is now offered as a service across SAP BTP ABAP Environment, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition for releases 2021 and higher. Joule speaks ABAP, and now it speaks more than ever before. See the details.

Upgrading your SAPUI5 Rich Text Editor: Beyond TinyMCE version 4

The Rich Text Editor is an SAPUI5 control for data input and editing. It is used to enter rich text with different styles, colors, and formatting. The Rich Text Editor allows users to format text and add different types of elements, such as images and hyperlinks. he SAPUI5 team further plans to permanently remove TinyMCE version 4 from the SAPUI5 code later in 2026. Learn how to adjust your code to avoid any problems.

Events

The future of UX is here. Are you ready for an AI -powered world?

At Sapphire 2026 I had the opportunity to speak with many customers and partners about their reaction to the way we are moving with our UX strategy. Generally, people are excited about the direction we are going and having more automation to free up time for deeper thought and analysis, but they are concerned about trusting AI to do things that might have significant business impact. Read my full analysis.

SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley focused on AI strategy and tactics for implementation

SAP Connect Day for UX in Silicon Valley connected customers with SAP experts and selected partners to discuss how AI will improve the lives of SAP Cloud ERP users. Read about highlights and where you can learn more about the topics we discussed.

Back issues from the past year

SAP Fiori Development Newsletter issue #40 (May 2026)

SAP Fiori Development newsletter March 2026 (issue #39)

SAP Fiori development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38)

SAP Fiori development newsletter November 2025 (issue #37)

SAP Fiori development newsletter September 2025 (issue #36)

SAP Fiori development newsletter July 2025 (issue #35)

entry.title: Re: Setting Up SAP Joule on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: A Technical Guide
entry.content:
@Arunrajj 

Joule setup in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition is mostly guided by SAP’s booster and BTP onboarding, not a lot of manual plumbing.

In simple terms:

  • You need the right BTP entitlements, SAP Build Work Zone, and Cloud Identity setup.

  • Run the Joule booster, which activates the needed communication scenario in the background.

  • Expose the Fiori launchpad content, register the S/4 system, and configure user attributes and IPS.

  • Once that’s done, Joule is available inside S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for supported use cases.

A few limits to keep in mind:

  • It works in English only for now.

  • It’s designed for one S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition instance.

  • You need browser support for iFrames and third-party cookies.

entry.title: From Silos to Strategy: What 20 Years of SAP Data Management Taught One Practitioner
entry.content:
Insights from a conversation with Rakesh Ramakrishnan, Principal Development Consultant at SAP and veteran SAP data management practitioner and consultant

When it comes to data management, there’s no shortage of frameworks, tools, and buzzwords competing for attention. But talk to someone who has spent two decades in the trenches of SAP implementations, and the noise falls away quickly. What's left is a set of hard-won principles every data practitioner should internalize before their next project kicks off.

I recently sat down with Rakesh Ramakrishnan, a longtime SAP practitioner who started his career as a C and Visual C++ programmer before an accidental encounter with an SAP customer in Malaysia in the year 2000 that changed his trajectory. Twenty years later, working as a development lead in the data management space in North America, Rakesh has a clear point of view on what separates successful data management implementations from the ones that stumble.

Start with the Customer's Vision—Not the Data

Ask Rakesh what a successful data management implementation looks like, and he doesn't start with tools or even data. He starts with the customer.

"It all starts with the customer's vision and long-term goal. To fulfill that strategy, what capabilities are required? Within those capabilities, what end-to-end processes are running? And when you come to the level of process, that is where you discover what sort of data is flowing between them."

Data is a mandatory ingredient of any successful implementation—but it's not the starting point. Skip business strategy, and you'll spend the rest of the project reworking things you shouldn't have built.

The Silo Mindset Is Still the #1 Killer of Data Programs

Rakesh's most direct warning was about how organizations think about data:

"Most people think about data in a siloed environment. If procurement is there, the procurement team is only looking at what data they want. They're not looking at where the data is coming from, who's consuming it, or whether something is missing that could impact subsequent processes."

The result? Failure that cascades through the entire customer vision. In SAP's world, he emphasized, "we never talk about silos—we always talk end-to-end process." Data governance, lineage, and design decisions must reflect that end-to-end reality.

Governance Must-Haves: Data as a Whole, Lineage, and Footprint

When asked which governance capabilities are non-negotiable, Rakesh was refreshingly clear:

"To me, it is data as a whole—master data and transactional data—along with data lineage and data footprint. That is what is more critical."

If you understand what data each process requires, where it originates, and which processes should receive it, you can pass only the necessary data to only the necessary processes. Overloading downstream processes with unnecessary data is a recipe for confusion and risk. Before diving into cataloging, classification, or business data fabric conversations, make sure your data lineage and footprint story is airtight.

The Real Lesson from a Successful Project: Step Out of the Comfort Zone

Rakesh's proudest project was a retail SAP S/4HANA implementation at a wholesale trade company, spanning sales, procurement, and finance. What made it stand out wasn't the technology, it was the method.

During the Explore phase, workshops ran from 8 a.m. through the afternoon. Rather than passively "taking data in," Rakesh introduced a collaborative flip-chart mechanic: each business domain maintained its own flip chart, and at the end of every workshop day, each domain presented processes, requirements, and gaps. The result was a unified picture of processes and the data flowing between them, giving the architect visibility into cross-topic dependencies.

His larger point:

"As a team member, it becomes our responsibility to step out of the comfort zone and think from the process perspective. Every rework has an impact on timelines. If you spend some time upfront, you reduce rework and unnecessary risks."

Data professionals who stay strictly in the "load and reload" lane will always be part of the problem. The ones who understand process become indispensable.

Get the Right People in the Room — Especially the End Users

Who should be part of the data conversation? Rakesh was emphatic: the end users (that’s you - the data professionals!).

"On the source side from the customer end, it is mostly the end users. The presence of the department head is also required, but it is primarily the end user, because he or she is the one playing around with this data."

The project mentioned earlier succeeded because business users and department heads were pulled in alongside subject matter experts to answer critical questions: Is this the right data? Is this information really mandatory?

Where the project had loopholes — unsurprisingly — was in data profiling and harmonization. Skipping these topics guarantees pain during execution.

Choose the Right Tool for the Right Job

One of the most candid parts of the conversation was about tool selection. Rakesh's favorite feature on the SAP S/4HANA side? The SAP Migration Cockpit.

"Migration Cockpit comes with standard templates in line with the back-end tables or structures. SAP gives you the right template so the data goes right into that particular thing."

But Migration Cockpit isn't the answer to everything. SAP Signavio provides the complete process picture.  And SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) is powerful and ever-evolving solution for AI and next-generation data scenarios.

The Foundation Comes Before the Autonomy

As the industry naturally is buzzing about the autonomous enterprise, Rakesh's closing thought was grounded:

"All concepts are good. We have all these beautiful tools. But the only thing required is properly structuring it out."

You can't have an autonomous enterprise without a data foundation. And you can't build that foundation without doing the unglamorous work: aligning to business strategy, breaking silos, enforcing lineage, involving end users, profiling and harmonizing data, and picking the right tool for each job.

Final Thoughts

If there’s one thread running through Rakesh’s twenty years of experience, it’s this: great data management is less about technology and more about awareness, awareness of business goals, of end-to-end processes, of who consumes what data, and which tools truly fit the problem.

For those of us implementing data management solutions, it is about having the discipline to slow down at the start, ask the right questions, and bring in the right people in order to build a data foundation that will actually support what comes next — whether that’s an SAP S/4HANA migration or the autonomous enterprise of tomorrow.

What’s the #1 lesson from your data & analytics/AI implementations? Drop it in the comments.

entry.title: Re: HELP! The database migration is too slow! #DMOVE2S4
entry.content:
Hello Franc!

Software Update Manager performs  latency checks itself (phases dbstat_*) and issues a warning in CHECKS.log if a high latency was measured. You can follow the numbers also in respective log file (DBSTAT.LOG).

The package size varies depending on the actual execution of Software Update Manager and its internal calculations. For throughput, I have seen testing in the past with around 4-8 kBytes (emulating batch load) in the context of distributed data centers (not related to DMOVE2S4).

All the best!

entry.title: Re: Variable Pay: how to get to the total sum of assignme...
entry.content: "this person had a change in the business goals during the year, so he was assigned to the bonus plan B" --> Business goals changes shouldn't dictate a change of bonus plan. Bonus plans should only change when the weighting between sections changes. If your weightings don't change then you should have 2 records in employee history hence two records when you open the worksheet with different business goals in each of them BUT both of them under Bonus Plan A.

entry.title: Error calling API_BUSINESS_PARTNER via Communication Arrangement in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am trying to consume the standard API_BUSINESS_PARTNER service from ABAP Cloud in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

I have already completed the following:

  • Created the Communication System
  • Created the Communication Arrangement using SAP_COM_0008

  • Activated the service
  • Generated a Service Consumption Model from the service metadata
  • Implemented the API call in ABAP Cloud

I am using:

lo_destination = cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_comm_arrangement(
comm_scenario = 'SAP_COM_0008'
comm_system_id = 'CS_EINVOICE_SAP_COM_0008'
service_id = 'API_BUSINESS_PARTNER_0001_IWSG'
).
 
However, when executing the code, I receive an error during destination creation / service consumption.

I downloaded the metadata and confirmed that the service contains entities such as:

A_BusinessPartner
A_BusinessPartnerAddress
A_AddressEmailAddress
A_AddressPhoneNumber
A_BusinessPartnerTaxNumber
 
 

My goal is to retrieve Customer / Business Partner details (name, address, email, phone and tax number) from ABAP Cloud.

Could anyone advise:

  1. How can I verify the correct value for service_id?
  2. Should the service_id be the Technical Service ID, Service Definition Name, or Inbound Service ID from the Communication Arrangement?
  3. Is there a recommended way in Public Cloud to consume API_BUSINESS_PARTNER from ABAP Cloud using Communication Arrangement and Service Consumption Model?
  4. Are there any additional authorizations or configuration steps required?
 

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you

entry.title: Re: Estimated Costs in Work Order Not Editable in Fiori
entry.content: The most likely cause is missing Business Function activation,  please check if Business Function LOG_EAM_CI_6 is active in your systems if not please activate it. Review SAP KBA 1641394 has details on how to activate the BF.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
1. db/schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
  Waiting;    // Session created, not yet started
  Playing;    // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
  Moving;     // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
  Blocked;    // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
  Finished;   // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
  key square      : Integer;        // 1–100
      snakeTo     : Integer;        // 0 if no snake on this square
      ladderTo    : Integer;        // 0 if no ladder on this square
      isDoubleHead: Boolean default false;  // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);    // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      name        : String(100);
      @readonly
      position    : Integer default 1;      // current square (1–100)
      @readonly
      TurnStatus  : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
      turnOrder   : Integer;                // 1 = goes first
      lastRoll    : Integer;               // last dice result
}

2. srv/game-service.cds

using { snakeladder } from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {

    // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
    @from: [ #Waiting ]
    @TO: #Playing
    action startTurn();

    @from: [ #Playing ]
    @TO: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns Integer;

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Waiting                  // transitions to next player in Week 3
    action confirmMove();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Blocked                  // double-headed snake bite
    action blockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Blocked ]
    @TO: $flow.previous            // restores pre-block status
    action unblockPlayer();

    @from: [ #Moving ]
    @TO: #Finished
    action winGame();
  }

  entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

I kept the original data

entry.title: Шлях до автономного підприємства | Agent-Led Toolchain Series
entry.content:
Деякі теми заслуговують на більше, ніж одну сесію.
Agent-Led Toolchain Series — це саме такий випадок: шість вебкастів, бізнес- та ІТ-перспективи, реальні кейси — все разом складає цілісну картину того, як агентно-керований підхід SAP веде організації до автономного підприємства.

Серія охоплює повний шлях RISE — від бізнес-стратегії до ІТ-виконання. Кожна сесія будується на попередній, тому рекомендуємо зберегти всі дати одразу.

Час вказано за Києвом | Мова — англійська.

  • 9 липня, 17:00 – 18:00 
    Agent-Led Toolchain Series: Accelerate ERP modernization and realize long-term business value with SAP's agent-led toolchain 
    Дізнайтеся, як організації — зокрема Nestlé — використовують агентно-керований підхід для оркестрації ERP-трансформації з більшою узгодженістю, операційною видимістю та гнучкістю на всьому шляху RISE. Ви дізнаєтеся, як прискорити трансформацію, покращити її видимість і забезпечити вимірювану бізнес-цінність.
  • 16 липня, 10:00 – 11:00 
    Agent-Led Toolchain Series: Orchestrating the Agent-Led Toolchain Across Your RISE Journey – From a Business Perspective 
    ERP-трансформація — це вже не лише технологічна ініціатива, а стратегічний бізнес-шлях. У цій сесії дізнайтеся, як пов'язати бізнес-пріоритети, процеси, дані та трансформаційні активності для більш цілісного та результативного проходження шляху RISE.
  • 22 липня, 10:00 – 11:00 
    Agent-Led Toolchain Series: Orchestrating the Agent-Led Toolchain Across Your RISE Journey – From an IT Perspective (Part 1) 
    ІТ-команди відіграють ключову роль у вирівнюванні бізнес-пріоритетів, технологічних ландшафтів та стратегій виконання. У першій частині ви дізнаєтесь, як покращити узгодженість трансформації, прискорити її виконання та зміцнити управління й гнучкість на шляху RISE.
  • 29 липня, 10:00 – 11:00 
    Agent-Led Toolchain Series: Orchestrating the Agent-Led Toolchain Across Your RISE Journey – From an IT Perspective (Part 2) 
    Продовження попередньої сесії з поглибленим розглядом практичних підходів до модернізації з більшою впевненістю, гнучкістю та довгостроковим бізнес-ефектом.
  • 5 серпня, 10:00 – 11:00 
    Agent-Led Toolchain Series: From Cloud ERP to Autonomous Enterprise – Aligning Business and IT for Continuous Improvement 
    Перехід на S/4HANA — це фундамент, а не фінішна лінія. Щоб розкрити повний потенціал S/4HANA та рухатися до автономного підприємства, організації мають узгодити бізнес і ІТ для підтримки безперервного вдосконалення, швидшого прийняття рішень та більшої операційної гнучкості. У п'ятому вебкасті серії дізнайтеся, як провідні організації виходять за межі міграції ERP — поєднуючи трансформаційні активності, операційну ефективність та безперервні інновації в масштабах підприємства.
  • 11 серпня, 10:00 – 11:00 
    Agent-Led Toolchain Series: Reimagine ERP Transformation with Migration and Modernization Assistants 
    Як AI-асистенти міграції та модернізації переосмислюють підхід до ERP-трансформації? Ця сесія розкриває, як інтелектуальні інструменти SAP допомагають усунути розрив між бізнесом і ІТ, підтримати безперервне вдосконалення та побудувати стійку основу для довгострокової трансформаційної цінності на шляху до автономного підприємства.

Це повна картина трансформації — від першого кроку міграції до автономного підприємства. Переходьте за посиланнями у назвах вебкастів, реєструйтесь заздалегідь і запрошуйте колег.

Стежте та діліться враженнями✨

entry.title: Re: unable to deploy the Value mapping Iflow
entry.content:
Hello,

The error indicates that the same value mapping relationship was defined more than once in the VM Artifact.  CPI requires each source-to-target mapping combinaton to be unique, so when it detects the same combination in more than one place, it treats it as a conflict and stops the deployment.

Open the Value Mapping artifact and search for WFS Status Ranking, EC Employment Status and remove the duplicate mapping before redeploying.

entry.title: Re: BTP + Joule integration
entry.content: Thanks for the perspective. However, my requirement is specifically about Joule acting as an event‑driven orchestrator in a utility context — not just reactive to human triggers. In meter‑to‑cash and outage workflows, Joule needs to consume events directly from Event Mesh, apply AI reasoning, and then trigger SAP Build Process Automation workflows. The answer doesn’t fully address how Joule integrates with Event Mesh, CAP, and Integration Suite to achieve this, nor how it operationalizes workflows in S/4HANA utility processes. Without that, the architecture remains incomplete for real‑world utility scenarios.

entry.title: Re: Event‑driven architecture
entry.content:
Thanks for the input. My requirement is specifically about large‑scale utility workflows where Event Mesh must do more than just route events — it needs schema governance, replay, and filtering to support AI services at scale.

The answer doesn’t fully explain how Joule or AI Core subscribe to Event Mesh topics, classify millions of meter events, and then trigger SAP Build Process Automation or S/4HANA actions. Without that integration detail, the solution doesn’t meet the operational needs of meter‑to‑cash and outage management in real utility landscapes.

Dear @azlanmn,

Yes, VIES validation is also available for SAP S/4HANA on-premise deployments. SAP provides dedicated on-premise documentation for Online Validation, where VIES is explicitly listed as a use case for validating VAT registration numbers

More details below:

SAP Help – Online Validation
SAP Help – VAT Information Exchange System (VIES)
SAP Help – Online Validation for Multiple Business Partners
SAP Help – Setting Up Direct Connection for VIES Validation

Please note that the configuration is deployment-specific. In Public Cloud the setup is done via SSCUI 105630, while in on-premise systems the customer should follow the corresponding on-premise customizing and technical connection setup

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Aditya Sai Rohith Kaki, and this is my journey into SAP
entry.content:
Hi everyone!

I’m Aditya Sai Rohith Kaki, currently pursuing an MBA in Marketing and IT at ICFAI Business School, Hyderabad.

If someone had asked me a few years ago where I’d be today, I probably wouldn’t have imagined introducing myself to a global SAP community.

I started my career in software testing, working with teams at Accenture and Flecks Labs, where I learned the importance of quality, teamwork, and solving problems with data. Later, during my MBA, I had the opportunity to intern with Varun Beverages (PepsiCo). Spending time in the market, interacting with retailers, analyzing sales data, and building dashboards showed me how technology and business come together to make better decisions.

That curiosity eventually brought me to SAP, and I’m excited to keep learning from this amazing community.  

What is your professional life dream?

My dream is simple to become someone who solves real business problems.

Whether that’s as a Business Consultant, Product Manager, or in another role, I want to bridge the gap between business and technology. I enjoy understanding how businesses work and finding smarter ways to improve processes using data and digital solutions. That’s one of the reasons SAP interests me so much.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

Five years from now, I hope to be working on large-scale digital transformation projects, helping businesses make better decisions through technology.

I also want to keep learning because technology changes every day, and I believe staying curious is one of the most valuable skills anyone can have.

What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why?

One experience I’ll always remember was my internship at Varun Beverages (PepsiCo).

Instead of sitting behind a desk, I spent weeks visiting retail stores, talking to shop owners, understanding customer preferences, analyzing competitors, and later converting those insights into Power BI dashboards.

It wasn’t just about numbers it was about seeing how small decisions in the market could make a big difference for a business. That experience made me appreciate the power of data-driven decision-making.  

What do you imagine is the future of AI and Enterprise Technology?

I don’t think AI will replace people.

I think it will help people make faster and better decisions.

The future belongs to professionals who understand both business and technology. Platforms like SAP will become even more intelligent, helping companies predict trends, automate repetitive work, and focus more on innovation. I’m excited to be part of that journey.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

One lesson my parents have always reminded me of is:

“Stay grounded, keep learning, and never stop putting in honest effort.”

Life doesn’t always go as planned, but consistency and patience eventually open doors. That’s something I try to carry with me every day.

And now I’d love to hear from you!

If you could learn one new skill that could shape your career over the next five years, what would it be and why?

Feel free to share your thoughts in the comments. I’d love to learn from your experiences too.

Looking forward to connecting with everyone in the SAP Community. Here’s to learning, growing, and building something meaningful together!

#HelloSAPCommunity #SAPCommunity #SAPUniversityAlliances #DigitalTransformation #SAP #AI #BusinessTechnology #LearningJourney

entry.title: Re: Title: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Automatic Payme...
entry.content:
Hi byd,

Short answer: The "payment order" behavior is controlled by the Payment Order Only indicator on the payment method at country/region level (SSCUI 101972). Remove it (or use a payment method that does not have it) so the run posts the payment document and clears the supplier item directly against the bank clearing/sub-account. Everything below is Public Cloud only.

1. Exactly which setting needs to change, and in which SSCUI?

SSCUI 101972 - Set Up Payment Methods for Each Country/Region for Payment Transactions. The trigger is the Payment Order Only indicator (classic field XNOPO) in the payment method's Posting Details. When it is set, the payment run creates a payment order and posts nothing, so the supplier item stays open and is locked against manual clearing — exactly the F1612 behavior you see. Remove the indicator to switch to direct posting.

Reference (concept): SAP Help – Payment Order. Error you are hitting is expected for payment-order items: KBA 3503165.

2. What follow-on settings must be maintained (e.g. document type for payment)?

As soon as Payment Order Only is off, the posting fields become relevant again and must be filled in the same activity (SSCUI 101972), Posting Details: the Document Type for Payment (and the clearing document type). With the indicator set these fields are greyed out, which is why they may currently be empty.

In the standard (no payment order) line: the run posts Supplier → Bank Clearing / bank sub-account and clears the supplier open item immediately; the sub-account is cleared later by the bank statement. This is the normal Public Cloud outgoing-payment flow. Reference: SAP Community (by SAP) – Automatic Supplier Payment Clearing in S/4HANA Public Cloud.

3. Impact on Electronic Bank Statement (EBS) clearing?

Yes — matching logic changes, and this is actually a simplification.

With payment order: EBS clears by matching the bank line to the payment order number (classic interpretation algorithm 029), and only then is the supplier item cleared.

With direct posting: the supplier item is already cleared at runtime, so EBS no longer needs the payment-order number. The statement only has to clear the bank clearing / sub-account against the main bank G/L, using your normal EBS posting rules/search string mapping. Make sure your processing rules resolve the outgoing payment to the sub-account - maintained in Manage Processing Rules - For Bank Statements app.

4. How to handle open items already blocked by an existing payment order?

You need to release them by removing the existing payment order first. The change to the payment method is not retroactive; items already tied to a payment order stay locked until that order is removed.

In Manage Automatic Payments (F0770), open the run that created the payment order and delete the payment order / payment media; the supplier items are then released and become selectable again for clearing (or clear them via the next run once the method is corrected). If payment media were already generated, deletion is blocked and must be handled first in the Manage Payment Media app. References: KBA 3168196, KBA 3374400.

Note to verify:

  • Country legal requirement: Payment Order Only is a legal requirement in some countries (items must stay open until the bank confirms). Confirm switching to direct posting is acceptable for that company code's country before changing it.

Summary: Turn off Payment Order Only in the country/region payment method (SSCUI 101972) and maintain the Document Type for Payment in its Posting Details, or assign a payment method without that indicator; then remove the existing payment order in F0770 to release the currently locked items. EBS then simply clears the bank sub-account instead of matching on the payment-order number. Verify the flag is editable for your (possibly SAP-delivered) payment method and that direct posting is legally acceptable for that country. I highly recommend you also self-study the documentation on SAP Help.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Where Did My Backups Go? Understanding Data Retention and .bacpac Files in SAP Commerce Cloud
entry.content:
Hi everyone!

If you work with SAP Commerce Cloud, you know that keeping infrastructure running at peak performance is a top priority. To maintain this efficiency and control infrastructure growth, SAP enforces a strict data retention strategy.

Today, let’s clarify exactly how the Cloud Portal treats different data retention types, from automatic backups to downloaded .bacpac files.

Understanding Your Backup and Export Options

Since SAP Commerce Cloud runs on Microsoft Azure infrastructure, managing database storage efficiently is critical. There are three distinct ways data is handled, and understanding the differences prevents unexpected data loss:

  • Standard Data Backups: These are the automated, full system backups managed by SAP. They are part of the core infrastructure and never expire.

  • Quick Data Backups: This is a self-service, point-in-time database restore mechanism available in the Cloud Portal. Note that this is purely for the database (it does not back up files). These temporary restores expire automatically after 35 days or immediately after a restore is performed.

  • Database Exports (.bacpac files): A .bacpac file is a fully decoupled logical copy containing your database schema and table data. This is not a standard self-service product functionality and is not listed in standard public documentation. To generate one, you must submit a Service Request for a "Database Export".

Why .bacpac Files Disappear Quickly

Once created via a Service Request, the .bacpac export is sent directly to your environment's hotfolder storage account so developers can download it locally. Because uncompressed database exports cause massive, unnecessary storage growth, SAP applies strict automated lifecycle rules to these temporary files.

Just like the Commerce Logs Blob Storage has a rigid, unchangeable 30 to 90-day retention policy, the hotfolder storage account enforces a 7-day retention policy for .bacpac files. After 7 days, the automated lifecycle system permanently purges them. If your team does not download the file within this window, you will need to submit a brand-new Service Request to export the database again.

How to Get the Files: Azure Storage Explorer & AzCopy

If you want an easier or faster way to grab your database exports and logs before they are permanently purged, you don't have to rely solely on manual portal downloads. You can use official Microsoft tools to connect directly to your storage accounts:

  • Microsoft Azure Storage Explorer: A user-friendly graphical interface (GUI) that lets you easily connect to your SAP Commerce Cloud Azure Blob storage, browse your containers, and download files with a few clicks.
  • AzCopy: A high-performance command-line utility designed for copying data to and from Azure Blob storage. It is perfect for moving large .bacpac files quickly and can easily be added to your automation scripts.

Important: If your team is planning a database extraction for long-term historical records, audit tracking, or intensive local offline development, make sure to capture and download the export file before the 7-day lifecycle window closes.

💡What is your strategy? Do you manually download your .bacpac files on time, or have you already automated this process?
Let’s swap ideas in the comments!

👉 Links for reference:

  • Data Backup and Restore 
  • Commerce Logs Blob Storage
  • Connecting to Microsoft Azure Blob Storage

entry.title: 11. SAP SuccessFactors HCM Implementation - Rehire Process Design for Historical Termination Records
entry.content:
This is part of an ongoing blog series on SAP SuccessFactors' HCM implementation. In this post, we explore how to design the Rehire Process for Historical Termination Records that are not part of EC. In the previous blog, Part 1, I covered rehire options in EC and ONB, requirements validation, dependencies, and rehire check options for existing terminated or inactive employees in Employee Central (EC) and Onboarding. The complex problem every SAP SuccessFactors HCM customer faces is how to detect rehires who are not in Employee Central.

 

This blog is part 2 – it discusses design options and recommendations for detecting historical rehires who are not in Employee Central.

 

Customer Requirement Scenario:

 

A customer has been running a legacy HR system for more than 25 years and is now implementing SAP SuccessFactors HCM. As part of the data migration strategy, only the top-of-stack employee data is being migrated to Employee Central (EC), which aligns with SAP's leading practice recommendation. As a result, approximately 25 years of terminated employee records will remain in the legacy system and will not be available in EC after go-live.

The customer requested a detailed solution design to identify, and process rehires for these historical terminated employees after the SAP SuccessFactors EC goes live.

As discussed in my previous blog, when a terminated employee record exists in Employee Central, the standard rehire checks in SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding (ONB) and Employee Central automatically detect a potential match and provide HR administrators with the option to Rehire on Old Employment or Rehire with New Employment, depending on the business scenario.

However, if the terminated employee record is not available in Employee Central, the system treats the individual as a new hire by default, resulting in the creation of new Person IDs, User IDs, Employment IDs, and Employment records. For organizations that want to preserve employee history, maintain compliance, or recognize prior service, this behavior may not meet their business requirements.

Based on my implementation experience, I have successfully designed and implemented three approaches to address this requirement. Among these, the first option is my preferred and most strongly recommended approach because it is simple, scalable, cost-effective, and aligns well with SAP SuccessFactors architecture.

 

Solution Design  1) Migrate historical terminated records with a minimum set of data/portlets:

The cleanest approach is migrating all termination records with a minimum set of data. As part of the migration, we identify terminated records and load them with a minimal data set after discussing with the business which attributes are critical; the remaining mandatory attributes will use default values. While this adds additional effort to the data migration scope, it makes the Rehire process very clean.

This is the recommended approach, in my view. If a customer has thousands of terminated records and migrating them as part of the Employee Central (EC) implementation requires significant effort, they could migrate them after EC is live. However, I strongly recommend doing it as part of the initial EC migration itself.

 

Since we are only going to load a few templates (Biographical, Personal, Address, Email, National ID, Employment, and Job Info) with a minimal set of real values and default values.

 

If you are migrating data from S/4HANA or SAP ERP HCM via Info Porter, you can set up a separate mapping template for terminated employees and load those records accordingly.

If you are migrating data manually using import templates or other migration tools, make sure you have the right logic in place to generate the correct data.

Data Migration Process for Terminated Records

Points to Remember: 

  • This solution adds extra effort to data migration, and that needs to be considered.
  • Bring only the top-of-stack termination record and add the hire record.
  • Try to populate only the minimum set of required fields in Job Info, use default values for foundation object attributes (Position, Department, Division, Pay Grade, Cost Center..etc).
  • If migrating all historical terminations is not an option, consider how far back you go for rehires. Based on that, migrate historical termination records.

 

Solution Design 2) Create an MDF object and load historical termination records:

This is another approach I have used in the past: when migrating historical termination records is not an option due to a high volume of records, we create a custom MDF object to maintain a minimum set of data. This includes attributes such as First Name, Last Name, Date of Birth, National ID, Termination Date, Email, and other minimum required fields.

 

  1. Migrate historical former employee data into a Historical Former Employees MDF object.
  2. If an external recruiting system is used, it can call the Historical Former Employees MDF Object OData API to identify potential rehires during the recruiting process.
  3. In SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting (RCM), a rehire check can be implemented using the MDF object to detect potential rehires before the hiring process progresses.
  4. Once onboarding is initiated, build a process to validate the employee against the Historical Former Employees MDF object, flag potential rehires, and trigger the appropriate action. This can be implemented using a business rule.
  5. For employees hired directly in Employee Central (EC), a business rule can be configured during the hire process to check the Historical Former Employees MDF object, flag potential rehires, and enable the HR administrator to take the appropriate rehire action.

 

Solution Design 3)Build a process to look up the external system where historical HR data resides:

 

This is the third option we have used to perform a rehire check. Assume the customer is moving from S4HANA to SAP SuccessFactors HCM, will use S4HANA for payroll, and will keep complete historical HR Data.

 

The above options are not feasible for various reasons, i have used this design to perform a lookup in the S4HANA system to check for rehire. If any matches are found, it returns the details with the Employee ID and other attributes. Based on this, you take action.

 

 

  • Create an OData or SOAP service in SAP S/4HANA that returns historical employee data based on input parameters. Expose the service through SAP API Management so that it can be securely consumed by external systems.
  • If an external recruiting system is used, it can call the SAP S/4HANA OData service to identify potential rehires during the recruiting process.
  • In SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting (RCM) or SmartRecruiters, a rehire Check can be implemented by calling the SAP S/4HANA OData service through SAP Integration Suite, triggered by Intelligent Services events.
  • Once onboarding is initiated, SAP Integration Suite can invoke the SAP S/4HANA OData service to validate whether the individual is a potential rehire, flag the record, and trigger the appropriate rehire action ( Rehire with old employment /New Employment).

entry.title: Re: SAP ABAP training advice please.
entry.content: Hi, No need of learning Java or Python , You can straight away jump into ABAP.

entry.title: Re: How to change the serial number user status RECG
entry.content:
Hi,

1) If you need to do it in a program, You can use BAPI_EQUI_GETSTATUS to get status and you can change user statuses using FM ALM_ME_EQUI_USTATUS_CHANGE.

2) Additionally, there is also an option in IQ02 screen.

IQ02-> Go to -> special serial number functions.
